name: Ragi Koozh
description: Ragi Koozh, also known as Kezhvaragu Koozh or Aadi Koozh, is a traditional South Indian fermented millet drink made with finger millet (ragi), raw rice and buttermilk. It is a healthy cooling food prepared especially during the Tamil month of Aadi. The fermentation process develops natural probiotics and a pleasant sour flavour. Nutritious, filling and refreshing, it is an excellent breakfast or summer meal that aids digestion and provides sustained energy.

steps.instruction: Pulse 2 tbsp raw rice in a blender until it reaches a coarse, rava-like texture. Do not grind into fine powder.
In a bowl, mix ¼ cup ragi flour with ½ cup water. Whisk thoroughly until smooth and completely lump-free.
Bring 1½ cups water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot.
Add the coarsely ground rice and cook on medium heat for 10 minutes until soft.
Lower the flame and slowly pour the ragi slurry into the pot while stirring continuously.
steps.tip: Cook on medium flame and keep stirring to avoid getting burnt at the bottom.

steps.instruction: Cook on low-medium heat for 15 minutes, stirring constantly. The mixture will thicken and turn glossy.
Add a pinch of salt and mix well.
steps.tip: Adding salt after cooking helps better fermentation.

steps.instruction: Cover the pot and allow the mixture to ferment at room temperature for 8 hours (preferably overnight).
After fermentation, the mixture becomes slightly sour, airy and thick.
Take the required portion of fermented ragi base. Add ½ cup buttermilk and mix well until smooth and drinkable.
steps.tip: Fermented ragi koozh thickens significantly — always dilute with buttermilk just before serving.

steps.instruction: Garnish with chopped shallots, green chilli, coriander leaves, raw mango and grated ginger.
steps.tip: You can add chopped items while cooking itself, or at the end for crunch.

steps.instruction: Serve chilled or at room temperature.
steps.tip: Ragi Koozh is best consumed fresh on the day of fermentation.

tips: Fermented ragi koozh thickens significantly — always dilute with buttermilk just before serving.
Adding salt after cooking helps better fermentation.
For babies, skip fermentation and garnishes. Serve fresh, warm ragi porridge instead.
Use freshly prepared homemade ragi powder for better texture and flavour.
Cook on medium flame and keep stirring to avoid getting burnt at the bottom.
The consistency should be semi-solid or medium liquid — adjust buttermilk accordingly.
You can add chopped items while cooking itself, or at the end for crunch.
Ragi Koozh is best consumed fresh on the day of fermentation.
